text: A customer support agent built with the Claude Agent SDK occasionally skips an identity verification step before processing refund requests, leading to unauthorized refunds in ~8% of interactions. The team has already added system-prompt instructions emphasizing verification. What is the most effective way to ensure the verification step always occurs before refund processing?

options.text: Implement a PreToolUse hook that blocks the process_refund tool until get_customer has returned a verified customer ID
Add more emphatic language to the system prompt requiring verification before any financial operation
Create a separate verification subagent that runs independently before the main support agent
Configure tool_choice to force the get_customer tool on every interaction

explanation: A PreToolUse hook is deterministic code that runs before a tool executes, so it can block process_refund until it confirms get_customer has returned a verified ID. Prompt-based guidance (B) is probabilistic and has a non-zero failure rate — unacceptable for a financial action. A separate subagent (C) adds complexity without guaranteeing ordering. Forcing get_customer on every turn (D) breaks normal conversation and still doesn't gate the refund tool specifically.

text: A multi-agent research system has a coordinator delegating to three subagents: web_search, document_analysis, and synthesis. The synthesis subagent produces reports lacking the web_search agent's findings, even though the coordinator receives all subagents' results correctly. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The coordinator is not passing the complete web_search findings into the synthesis subagent's prompt context
The synthesis subagent's system prompt needs more explicit instructions about including all sources
The web_search subagent returns results in a format incompatible with the synthesis subagent
The synthesis subagent needs the web_search tool to verify findings independently

explanation: Subagents run in isolated contexts — they do not automatically inherit the coordinator's conversation or other subagents' outputs. If the coordinator doesn't explicitly include the web_search findings in the synthesis subagent's prompt, that information simply isn't available to it. Better prompting (B) can't surface data the subagent never received; a format issue (C) is unlikely since the coordinator parsed the results fine; giving synthesis its own search tool (D) violates least privilege and re-does work.

text: An architect is designing an agentic loop with the Claude API that must handle tool calls, detect completion, and manage errors. Which combination of stop_reason values correctly identifies when to continue the loop versus exit?

options.text: Continue on 'tool_use' or 'end_turn'; exit on 'max_tokens' or 'refusal'
Continue on 'tool_use' or 'max_tokens'; exit on 'end_turn' or 'stop_sequence'
Continue on 'tool_use'; exit only on 'end_turn'
Continue on 'tool_use'; exit on 'end_turn', 'max_tokens', 'refusal', or 'stop_sequence'

explanation: The loop should continue only when Claude wants to run a tool ('tool_use'); every other terminal stop_reason means stop and handle it: 'end_turn' (done), 'max_tokens' (truncated — handle/retry), 'refusal' (declined), 'stop_sequence' (hit a custom stop). (Note: server-side tools can also return 'pause_turn', which you resume.) B is wrong because 'max_tokens' is truncation, not a tool request; C ignores error/limit conditions.

text: A code-review system generates code and then validates it. When the same Claude session both generates code and reviews it, the reviews are less critical than when a separate session does the review. What architectural change most effectively addresses this bias?

options.text: Add stronger instructions in the review prompt to be critical and thorough
Use a separate Claude instance without the generation context to perform the review
Implement a multi-pass review within the same session with explicit self-critique prompts
Increase the temperature parameter during the review phase to encourage diverse perspectives

explanation: A reviewer that carries the generation context tends to rationalize its own work, so an independent instance with a clean context gives a more objective review. Stronger instructions (A) don't remove the underlying context bias; staying in the same session (C) keeps the contaminating context; temperature (D) affects randomness, not objectivity — and on current Claude models temperature isn't even an available knob.

text: A coordinator manages three research subagents investigating 'AI applications in creative industries.' All three covered visual arts extensively but missed music, creative writing, and film entirely. Each subagent executed its assigned task correctly and thoroughly. What is the root cause of this gap?

options.text: The subagents need larger context windows to cover more topics
The subagents should have web search tools to discover additional creative domains
The synthesis step should have identified and requested additional research on missing topics
The coordinator's task decomposition was too narrow, limiting scope to visual arts subtopics

explanation: When subagents do their assigned work correctly but the overall output has coverage gaps, the failure is upstream at task decomposition: the coordinator carved the problem into three visual-arts subtopics instead of distributing across the full creative-industries space. Context size (A) doesn't affect assigned scope; tools (B) don't fix scoping; a synthesis fix (C) only patches the symptom after the narrow research is done.

text: An architect needs two analysis paths that explore different hypotheses from the same shared baseline data. The paths must be independent but start from identical context. Which capability best supports this?

options.text: Create two separate sessions and manually copy the baseline data into each
Use session forking to create independent branches from the shared baseline
Use agent teams with a shared task list to coordinate the investigation
Run both analyses sequentially in the same session with context-separation prompts

explanation: Forking a session produces independent branches that start from identical accumulated context but then diverge — exactly the 'shared baseline, independent exploration' pattern. Manually copying into two sessions (A) is error-prone and risks drift. Agent teams (C) are for collaborative, communicating work, not isolated divergent exploration. Sequential runs in one session (D) lose parallelism and risk cross-contamination between the two analyses.

text: A support agent must handle multi-concern messages (e.g., a billing dispute AND a feature question in one message). Currently it addresses only the first concern. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Instruct the agent to process both concerns in sequence within a single response
Route the message to two different specialized agents simultaneously
Decompose the multi-concern request into distinct items and investigate each in parallel
Ask the customer to submit separate tickets for each concern

explanation: Explicitly decomposing the message into separate concern-items and handling each (in parallel) ensures full coverage and avoids the attention-dilution that causes later concerns to be dropped. Sequential handling in one response (A) is prone to the same dilution; blind dual-routing (B) skips the decomposition that identifies all concerns; pushing the work back to the customer (D) is a poor experience and doesn't solve the technical gap.

text: Using Claude Agent SDK hooks, a developer wants to enforce that refunds over $500 require manager approval. Which hook configuration correctly implements this?

options.text: A PostToolUse hook on process_refund that reverses transactions over $500
A SessionStart hook that sets a global $500 limit for all financial operations
A system-prompt instruction specifying the $500 threshold with examples
A PreToolUse hook on process_refund that denies the call when the amount exceeds $500

explanation: A PreToolUse hook intercepts the call before it runs, inspects the amount argument, and denies (with feedback) when it exceeds $500 — stopping the refund before any money moves. PostToolUse (A) only fires after the refund already executed. A SessionStart hook (B) can't evaluate per-call arguments. A prompt instruction (C) is probabilistic and unsuitable for a hard financial control.

text: A team resumes a Claude Code session after making significant code changes outside the session. The agent gives recommendations based on the previous, now-stale code state. What is the most reliable approach for accurate responses?

options.text: Use --resume with the session ID and immediately ask Claude to re-read all changed files
Fork the previous session and provide a diff of all changes since the last interaction
Use --continue to load the most recent session with updated file context
Start a fresh session with a structured summary of prior findings and the current code state

explanation: When code has changed substantially outside the session, the old session's tool results and reasoning are based on outdated files. Starting fresh with a concise summary of prior findings plus the current state gives Claude a clean, accurate basis. Resuming or continuing (A, C) re-loads a reasoning chain anchored to stale code; forking with a diff (B) still carries the stale baseline context forward.

text: A support agent handles tickets ranging from simple password resets (70% of volume) to complex multi-system outage investigations (5%). Should it use adaptive decomposition or a fixed sequential pipeline?

options.text: Fixed sequential pipeline — apply the same thorough process to every ticket for consistency
A single adaptive agent with a mandatory initial classification step before any processing
Two separate agents: one fixed pipeline for simple tickets, one adaptive for complex tickets
Adaptive decomposition — generate subtasks dynamically based on what's discovered at each step

explanation: When complexity varies widely, adaptive decomposition lets simple tickets resolve in one or two steps while complex ones trigger deeper, dynamically-generated investigation — matching effort to the task. A fixed pipeline (A) wastes work on the 70% simple cases. Two separate agents (C) add operational overhead and a routing decision. A mandatory classification gate (B) is close but unnecessarily rigid — an adaptive agent can scale depth without a hard gate.

text: An MCP server exposes 18 tools to a support agent. The agent frequently confuses get_order_status with get_order_details and check_shipping with track_package. What is the most effective solution?

options.text: Add retry logic that detects incorrect tool selection and re-routes to the correct tool
Use tool_choice to force specific tools based on keywords in the customer message
Consolidate overlapping tools and expand descriptions with clear differentiators
Provide few-shot examples of correct tool selection for each disambiguation case

explanation: Too many tools plus thin, overlapping descriptions is the root cause of misrouting — Claude selects tools primarily from their descriptions. Consolidating near-duplicate tools and writing descriptions that state exactly when to use each (and when not to) fixes the cause. Retry/re-routing (A) treats the symptom; keyword-forced tool_choice (B) bypasses Claude's reasoning and is brittle; few-shot examples (D) help but don't fix the structural problem of 18 vaguely-described tools.

text: An MCP tool for customer returns accepts a JSON object with nested fields (order_id, return_reason, item_conditions, refund_method). Agents frequently malform the nested item_conditions array. Which tool-definition improvement most reduces input errors?

options.text: Add validation inside the tool that rejects malformed inputs with helpful error messages
Include input examples in the tool definition showing correctly structured nested objects
Simplify the tool to accept only flat string parameters instead of nested objects
Split the tool into separate tools for each parameter to eliminate nesting

explanation: Concrete examples of correctly-structured input are the most effective way to get Claude to produce well-formed nested arguments — they show the exact shape rather than describing it. In-tool validation (A) only catches errors after they happen; flattening (C) sacrifices the data model; splitting into many tools (D) causes tool-proliferation that itself degrades selection. (A robust design often pairs B with A as a safety net.)

text: A document-extraction pipeline must always call extract_metadata first, but subsequent steps should let Claude choose tools based on content. Which tool_choice sequence is correct?

options.text: Use {"type":"tool","name":"extract_metadata"} on the first request, then switch to "auto"
Use "any" on the first request to guarantee a tool call, then "auto"
Use "auto" throughout and instruct the model to always start with extract_metadata
Use "none" on the first request to let Claude plan, then "auto"

explanation: Forcing the specific tool ({type:'tool', name:'extract_metadata'}) on the first request guarantees that exact tool runs first; switching to 'auto' afterward lets Claude reason about subsequent calls. 'any' (B) guarantees some tool but not which one; 'auto' + a prompt instruction (C) is only probabilistic; 'none' (D) forbids tools on turn one, the opposite of the requirement.

text: A multi-agent system's web_search subagent times out against an external API. The coordinator receives a generic 'Operation failed' and terminates the whole workflow. How should error handling be improved?

options.text: The coordinator should catch all errors and silently proceed without the web results
The subagent should automatically retry three times before reporting failure
The subagent should return structured error context: failure type, attempted query, partial results, and alternative approaches
The system should fall back to cached results from previous queries

explanation: Structured error context lets the coordinator make an informed recovery decision — retry, delegate elsewhere, or proceed with partial results — instead of aborting on an opaque failure. Blind retries (B) help only for transient errors and still give the coordinator no decision context. Silently proceeding (A) hides real failures. Cached fallback (D) risks serving stale or irrelevant data without signaling that it did so.

text: A team wants to share MCP server configurations across all developers while letting individuals also use personal experimental MCP servers. Where should each configuration go?

options.text: Team config in CLAUDE.md; personal config in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
Team config in .mcp.json at the project root; personal config in user-level settings
Team config in .claude/settings.json; personal config in environment variables
Both in .mcp.json with conditional loading based on the developer's username

explanation: Project-scoped MCP servers belong in a version-controlled .mcp.json at the repo root so the whole team gets them; personal/experimental servers belong in the developer's user-level config, which isn't committed. CLAUDE.md (A) is for instructions/memory, not MCP server definitions. Settings + env vars (C) and username-conditional loading (D) are non-standard and brittle.

text: A monorepo needs different coding conventions for React component files, API route files, and DB migration files, each scattered across many directories. What is the most maintainable way to scope conventions by file type?

options.text: Path-scoped rule files that match each file type by glob pattern, loaded only when editing matching files
Separate CLAUDE.md files in every directory that contains these file types
All conventions in the root CLAUDE.md under clear section headers for each file type
Custom slash commands for switching between convention modes

explanation: Glob-scoped rules load the right conventions only when a matching file is being edited, regardless of which directory it lives in — ideal for file types scattered across a monorepo, and it keeps irrelevant context out. Per-directory CLAUDE.md files (B) require duplication and miss scattered files. A monolithic root CLAUDE.md (C) loads every convention regardless of the file in play. Slash commands (D) require manual switching.

text: A team wants a reusable Claude Code skill for database migrations that runs in an isolated context (so verbose migration logs don't pollute the main conversation) and has access only to the Bash and Read tools. Which SKILL.md frontmatter achieves this?

options.text: An isolated/forked execution context, allowed-tools limited to [Bash, Read], and model-invocation disabled
An isolated/forked execution context with allowed-tools limited to [Bash, Read]
isolation: true, tools: [Bash, Read], disable-model-invocation: true
An isolated context with allowed-tools [Bash, Read, Write]

explanation: Running the skill in a forked/isolated sub-context keeps its verbose output out of the main conversation, and restricting allowed-tools to exactly [Bash, Read] enforces the requested scope. Disabling model invocation (A) would stop the model from triggering the skill — not asked for. Option C uses field names that aren't the real ones. Option D adds Write, which wasn't requested. (Exact frontmatter keys vary by Claude Code version — verify against current docs.)

text: An invoice-extraction system uses a JSON schema. Some invoices lack certain fields (e.g., purchase-order numbers). How should the schema handle potentially missing fields to prevent Claude from fabricating values?

options.text: Make all fields required and instruct Claude to use 'N/A' for missing values
Use enum fields with an 'unknown' option for fields that might be missing
Make potentially-absent fields nullable so Claude can return null instead of guessing
Add post-processing validation to detect and remove likely-fabricated values

explanation: Making a field nullable gives the model a legitimate way to say 'not present in the source' (null) instead of inventing a plausible value. Forcing a required string with 'N/A' (A) still pressures the model to emit something and pollutes the type. Enums (B) are for fixed value sets, not presence/absence. Post-processing (D) tries to detect fabrication after it happens rather than preventing it.

text: A team uses the Message Batches API for overnight compliance reports (non-blocking) and wants to also use it for pre-merge code-review checks in CI to save cost. What is the correct recommendation?

options.text: Use batch processing for the overnight reports only; use real-time API calls for pre-merge checks
Use batch processing for both — the 50% savings justify the CI delay
Use real-time API for both — batch processing only helps above 100K requests
Use batch processing for pre-merge checks only since they benefit from structured output

explanation: The Batches API trades latency for ~50% cost: it has no latency SLA and can take up to ~24 hours, which is fine for overnight reports but unacceptable for a blocking CI check where developers wait for feedback. So batch the reports and use real-time calls for pre-merge. B would stall the dev workflow; C invents a 100K-request floor that doesn't exist; D reverses the appropriate use cases.

text: A code-review tool has high false positives — flagging correct error handling as 'missing error handling' and intentional type assertions as 'unsafe casts.' Adding 'be more conservative' didn't help. What most effectively reduces false positives?

options.text: Add a confidence threshold that suppresses findings below 90% confidence
Increase the context window to give Claude more surrounding code
Provide 2–4 few-shot examples of the specific false-positive patterns and the correct non-flagging behavior
Use tool_choice to force a specific static-analysis tool instead of Claude's judgment

explanation: Targeted few-shot examples that show the exact patterns being mis-flagged and the correct (non-flagging) behavior teach the model to generalize and stop over-reporting — more effective than vague 'be conservative' instructions. Self-reported confidence thresholds (A) are poorly calibrated; more context (B) doesn't fix the reasoning error; forcing an external analyzer (D) removes Claude's review capability entirely.

text: A CI/CD pipeline uses Claude Code for automated code review but hangs during execution because Claude is waiting for interactive input. What is the correct fix?

options.text: Set a CLAUDE_CI=true environment variable to enable headless mode
Add the -p (--print) flag to run Claude Code non-interactively
Redirect stdin from /dev/null to prevent interactive prompts
Use a --daemon flag to run Claude Code as a background process

explanation: The -p / --print flag runs Claude Code in non-interactive mode: it processes the prompt, prints the result, and exits without waiting for input — exactly what CI needs. The other options reference flags/env vars that aren't the documented mechanism; redirecting stdin (C) is a fragile workaround that doesn't change Claude Code's interactive behavior.

text: During a long debugging session in Claude Code, the agent starts giving vague, generic responses like 'this follows typical patterns' instead of referencing the specific classes and methods it examined earlier. What is happening and what should you do?

options.text: Context degradation from an extended session — use /compact and persist key findings in scratchpad files
The model's temperature is rising over time — restart with a lower temperature
The model is rate-limited — wait a few minutes before continuing
The agent needs more explicit instructions — add detailed debugging steps to the system prompt

explanation: Vague, 'typical-patterns' answers replacing specific references are a classic sign of context degradation in a very long session. Compacting frees context, and persisting key findings (file paths, hypotheses, decisions) to scratchpad files lets them survive context boundaries. Temperature (B) doesn't drift during a session (and isn't a knob on current models); rate limiting (C) doesn't change answer quality; more instructions (D) add to an already-overloaded context.

text: A support agent resolves 55% of tickets but frequently escalates simple password resets while handling complex billing disputes itself. How should you improve escalation accuracy?

options.text: Add a confidence-score threshold where the agent self-reports certainty below 70% and escalates
Implement an ML classifier outside Claude to make escalation decisions
Add explicit escalation criteria with few-shot examples of when to escalate vs. resolve autonomously
Restrict the agent to only handle password resets and escalate everything else

explanation: Miscalibrated escalation is best fixed with explicit, written criteria plus few-shot examples showing the correct decision for each scenario type — this gives the model concrete decision boundaries. Self-reported confidence (A) is poorly calibrated; a separate ML classifier (B) is heavy engineering for a prompt-level problem; restricting scope (D) throws away most of the agent's value.

text: A data-extraction system shows 97% aggregate accuracy, but one vendor's invoices have a 23% error rate on 'tax_amount'. The current evaluation only checks aggregate accuracy across all vendors. What is the most effective improvement?

options.text: Lower the overall accuracy threshold from 97% to 90% to account for the problematic vendor
Add more training examples from the problematic vendor to the system prompt
Create a separate extraction pipeline for the problematic vendor
Use stratified sampling that measures accuracy by document type AND by field

explanation: Aggregate accuracy hides concentrated failures. Stratifying by document type and by field surfaces exactly these hidden pockets (a specific vendor's tax_amount) so you can act on them. Lowering the threshold (A) just hides the problem; adding examples (B) may help generation but doesn't fix the measurement gap; a separate pipeline (C) is premature before you understand the full distribution of issues.

text: After Claude responds with a tool_use block, how must you send the tool result back?

options.text: A user message with a tool_result content block (containing the tool_use_id and content), placed before any text blocks
A separate 'tool' role message with the tool name, result, and a reference to the call ID
An assistant message with a tool_result block appended after the tool_use block
A user message with the tool result as a plain text string referencing the tool name

explanation: Tool results go in a user message as tool_result blocks keyed by tool_use_id, and those blocks must come first in the content array (before any text) or the API returns a 400. There is no 'tool' role (B) — only user/assistant. Results don't go in an assistant message (C), and a plain string (D) lacks the tool_use_id correlation the API requires.

text: A document pipeline needs Claude to output JSON conforming to a specific schema. Prompt instructions alone occasionally yield malformed JSON with missing fields. What most reliably guarantees schema-compliant output?

options.text: Add JSON formatting examples in the system prompt with explicit field requirements
Use tool_use with a JSON schema and tool_choice forcing the extraction tool
Post-process Claude's output with a parser that fills defaults for missing fields
Use output_config.format with type json_schema (strict)

explanation: Structured outputs via output_config.format with a json_schema constrain generation to the schema at the response level — the strongest guarantee. A forced tool with a schema (B) also yields schema-valid output but at the tool-call level rather than the response. Prompt examples (A) are probabilistic; post-processing (C) fixes problems after they occur instead of preventing them.

text: A monorepo has frontend (React), backend (Express), and shared library packages, each needing context-specific instructions. What is the optimal CLAUDE.md configuration?

options.text: A single root CLAUDE.md with all instructions organized by package in sections
Root CLAUDE.md with universal standards plus subdirectory CLAUDE.md files for package-specific conventions
Only .claude/rules/ files with glob patterns targeting each package's file types
A user-level CLAUDE.md containing all project-specific instructions

explanation: The CLAUDE.md hierarchy is designed for this: a root file for universal standards plus per-package subdirectory CLAUDE.md files that add context when working in those directories. A single root file (A) loads everything regardless of context. Glob rules (C) work for file-type conventions but not package-level context. A user-level file (D) isn't shared with the team. (Glob rules and subdirectory files can also be combined.)

text: A synthesis subagent occasionally needs fact-checking. 85% of fact-checks are simple lookups it could do itself; 15% need complex verification via the coordinator. What tool-distribution approach is recommended?

options.text: Remove all fact-checking from the synthesis agent and route everything through the coordinator
Give the synthesis agent full access to all research tools to avoid bottlenecks
Give the synthesis agent a scoped verify_fact tool for simple lookups; route complex checks through the coordinator
Create a dedicated fact-checking subagent that handles all verification regardless of complexity

explanation: Least privilege with a pragmatic exception: give each agent the tools its primary role needs, plus a narrowly-scoped tool for high-frequency cross-role needs. A scoped verify_fact tool handles the common 85% locally while complex cases still escalate. Routing everything through the coordinator (A) bottlenecks simple lookups; full tool access (B) violates least privilege; a dedicated subagent (D) adds coordination overhead for trivial checks.

text: A coordinator wants to spawn multiple research subagents simultaneously to investigate different aspects of a question. How should it trigger parallel subagent execution?

options.text: Make sequential Task tool calls, one per subagent, with a 'parallel: true' flag
Configure the system prompt with a parallelism parameter specifying concurrent agent count
Use the batch processing API to submit all subagent requests simultaneously
Emit multiple Task tool calls in a single coordinator response

explanation: Claude supports parallel tool calling: when a single response contains multiple tool_use (Task) blocks, the harness can execute them concurrently. So the coordinator emits several Task calls in one response. There is no 'parallel: true' flag (A) or system-prompt parallelism parameter (B); the Batches API (C) is for independent, latency-tolerant requests, not coordinated live subagents.

text: A codebase-migration project requires investigating the legacy system, designing the new architecture, implementing changes, and writing tests. The phases are highly interdependent and the workers need to discuss findings. Subagents or agent teams?

options.text: Agent teams — teammates share findings, claim tasks from a shared list, and communicate directly
Subagents — each handles one phase and reports results back to the coordinator
A single agent session with extended context to maintain coherence across all phases
Prompt chaining with separate sessions per phase, passing summaries between them

explanation: When work is interdependent and requires back-and-forth, a collaborative agent-team model (shared task list, direct messaging) fits better than the report-back-only subagent pattern (B). A single long session (C) risks context degradation across all phases; prompt chaining across isolated sessions (D) loses cross-phase context and real-time coordination. (Verify the exact team primitives against current Claude Code docs.)

text: When tuning the effort parameter across a multi-agent system, which combination is most appropriate?

options.text: max for coordinator reasoning, high for primary analysis agents, low for simple data-fetching subagents
high for all agents to ensure consistent quality
low for the coordinator to minimize cost, max for analysis subagents
medium for all agents with manual temperature adjustments per task type

explanation: Match effort to the cognitive load of each role: high/max for the coordinator's planning and the heavy analysis, low for mechanical work like data fetching where speed and cost matter more. Uniform high (B) wastes tokens on trivial tasks; inverting it (C) starves the coordinator of reasoning; uniform medium plus temperature (D) ignores per-role tuning, and temperature isn't a knob on current models.

text: A financial-extraction pipeline uses a validation-retry loop. After three retries, 'total_amount' still fails because the extracted line items (which are correct) don't sum to the stated total. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: The retry loop needs more iterations to converge
The schema must be simplified to reduce extraction complexity
The retries are ineffective because the discrepancy is in the source document, not the extraction
Claude needs a higher effort level to do accurate arithmetic during extraction

explanation: If the individual line items are extracted correctly but don't sum to the stated total, the source document itself is inconsistent — retries can't fix data that's wrong at the source. More iterations (A) won't converge on a 'correct' value that doesn't exist; simplifying the schema (B) or raising effort (D) doesn't address a source-data problem. The right move is to flag the discrepancy, not keep retrying.

text: A research agent loses source attribution during summarization: the final report has statistics with no source, and conflicting data points are silently resolved by picking one value. What design change addresses both issues?

options.text: Add post-processing that uses web search to re-attribute claims
Require subagents to return structured data with claim-to-source mappings, and annotate conflicts with attribution rather than silently picking one value
Increase the context window to prevent information loss during summarization
Store all raw sources in a database and link report claims to source IDs

explanation: Carrying explicit claim-to-source mappings through the pipeline preserves provenance, and annotating conflicts (showing both values with sources) avoids silent, arbitrary resolution. Re-attributing after the fact (A) is unreliable; a bigger context window (C) doesn't address the structural loss during summarization; a source database (D) is extra infrastructure that still doesn't fix how the summary handles attribution and conflicts.

text: A developer's response includes both a text block and a tool_use block. When sending the tool result, they place a text block before the tool_result block in the user message and get a 400 error. What is wrong?

options.text: Text blocks are not allowed in messages that contain tool_result blocks
The tool_result must be sent in a separate message from any text content
tool_result blocks must come first in the user message's content array, before any text blocks
The original tool_use block must be included in the tool_result message

explanation: The API requires tool_result blocks to appear first in the content array; putting a text block ahead of them triggers a 400. Text blocks can coexist with tool_result (so A and B are wrong) — they just can't precede it. The tool_use block belongs in the prior assistant message, not echoed into the tool_result message (D).

text: A Claude Code skill for database migrations should accept a migration name as a parameter and use it in the skill's instructions. Which feature enables this?

options.text: A required-params field in the SKILL.md frontmatter
A custom input_schema JSON definition in the frontmatter
Environment variables set before invocation via a shell frontmatter field
The $ARGUMENTS substitution variable in the skill body, with argument-hint in frontmatter to prompt for it

explanation: Skill/command bodies support argument substitution ($ARGUMENTS for the full string, and positional $1, $2…), and an argument-hint in frontmatter prompts the user for the value when it isn't supplied. Skills don't use a required-params field (A) or input_schema (B); a shell frontmatter field (C) is for setup, not parameter passing. (Confirm exact field names against current Claude Code docs.)

text: An MCP tool returns a transient timeout on its first call, but the agent treats all errors identically and abandons the operation. How should the tool's error response be improved for smarter recovery?

options.text: Return structured error metadata including errorCategory, an isRetryable boolean, and a description distinguishing transient from permanent failures
Return a generic is_error: true with a human-readable message
Implement automatic retry inside the tool, hiding transient errors from the agent
Log all errors externally and return success with empty results

explanation: Structured error metadata (category + isRetryable + description) lets the agent decide intelligently: retry transient failures, escalate permanent ones. A generic error (B) gives no recovery signal. Hiding retries inside the tool (C) removes the agent's ability to reason about failures and can mask systemic problems. Returning fake success (D) is an anti-pattern that corrupts downstream reasoning.

text: A security-audit skill produces verbose logging that fills the main conversation context and crowds out earlier context. Which skill configuration prevents this?

options.text: Set max_tokens in the skill frontmatter to limit output length
Add logging: false to suppress verbose tool output
Run the skill in a forked/isolated sub-agent context so only its final result returns to the main conversation
Use the Explore subagent instead of a custom skill

explanation: Running the skill in an isolated/forked context keeps its verbose intermediate output out of the main conversation — only the summarized result comes back. max_tokens (A) and a logging flag (B) aren't the right frontmatter mechanisms for this. Switching to the Explore subagent (D) replaces the skill rather than configuring it to isolate output. (Verify the exact frontmatter key against current docs.)

text: An architect needs to see which Claude Code memory files are currently loaded in a session, to debug why some instructions seem ignored. Which command shows this?

options.text: /status
/config
/memory — shows which memory (CLAUDE.md) files are loaded in the current session
/debug

explanation: /memory shows the CLAUDE.md memory files loaded at the various levels (user, project, subdirectory), which is exactly how you debug instructions that aren't taking effect. The others aren't the command for inspecting loaded memory.

text: A long customer-support conversation is nearing the context-window limit. You must keep operating without losing critical transaction details (order numbers, refund amounts, commitments) from earlier. What is recommended?

options.text: Increase max_tokens to expand the context window
Extract transactional facts into a persistent 'case facts' block and use compaction to summarize older conversation
Start a new conversation and ask the customer to repeat the issue
Enable streaming to reduce the conversation's memory footprint

explanation: Pull the must-keep facts into a structured block that survives summarization, and let compaction condense the older conversational filler — you preserve the critical details while freeing space. max_tokens (A) controls output length, not the context window; restarting (C) is a poor customer experience; streaming (D) affects delivery, not context usage.

text: A team wants to use the Explore subagent in Claude Code to understand a large codebase before making changes. What are its key characteristics?

options.text: It uses a lightweight model (Haiku) with read-only access, isolates verbose output, and returns summaries
It uses a high-capability model with full write access for comprehensive exploration
It runs in the same context as the main agent but with enhanced search
It spawns multiple parallel sub-agents to explore different parts simultaneously

explanation: The Explore subagent is a focused, read-only discovery agent on a lightweight (Haiku) model that isolates its verbose searching from the main conversation and returns concise summaries — preserving the main context. It isn't a high-capability writer (B), doesn't share the main context (C), and is a single focused agent rather than a parallel fan-out (D).

text: A coordinator must pass findings from a web_search subagent to a synthesis subagent. The web_search agent returns a large amount of raw data (full page contents, URLs, timestamps, metadata). What is the best practice for structuring this transfer?

options.text: Use a structured format that separates content from metadata, including source URLs and page references
Pass the complete raw data to preserve fidelity
Have the coordinator summarize findings into a brief paragraph before passing them on
Store raw data in a shared database and pass only record IDs

explanation: A structured format that separates the actual content from its metadata (source URLs, page numbers) preserves provenance while keeping the payload lean and consumable. Passing all raw data (B) bloats context; pre-summarizing into prose (C) risks losing detail the synthesis step needs; a shared database with IDs (D) adds infrastructure the synthesis agent may not be able to query.

text: Using extended/adaptive thinking together with tool use, a developer strips all thinking blocks from the conversation before sending the next request, and the API errors. What rule was violated?

options.text: Thinking blocks must always be preserved in the full conversation history forever
Thinking blocks can only be removed after the entire conversation ends
Thinking is incompatible with tool use and must be disabled during tool calls
Thinking blocks must be returned alongside their corresponding tool results; they can be dropped only after those tool results have been processed

explanation: When thinking and tool use are active together, the thinking block(s) tied to a turn's tool_use must be echoed back with that turn's tool results on the same model — removing them mid-flight breaks the turn and errors. They can be dropped on later turns once those tool results have been processed. So A/B are too strict and C is simply wrong (thinking works with tool use).

text: An agent harness is running a conversation that exceeds ~150,000 input tokens and wants to automatically summarize older context while keeping recent turns. Which API feature handles this?

options.text: A token-limit parameter that auto-truncates messages at 150K
Automatic prompt caching that compresses repeated content
A client-side sliding window that drops the oldest messages
Server-side compaction (beta) configured with a trigger threshold

explanation: Server-side compaction (a beta feature, header compact-2026-01-12) automatically summarizes earlier context when input tokens cross a configurable threshold, emitting a compaction block that subsequent requests build on. There's no auto-truncate token parameter (A); prompt caching (B) reduces cost on repeated prefixes but doesn't shrink context; a client-side sliding window (C) is manual and bluntly drops possibly-important turns.

text: An MCP server needs API tokens for authentication. The tokens must stay out of version control but be available to all team members. How should this be configured in .mcp.json?

options.text: Hardcode the tokens directly in .mcp.json
Use environment-variable expansion like ${API_TOKEN} in .mcp.json, with each developer setting the variable locally
Store tokens in a separate gitignored .mcp-secrets.json file
Put tokens in user-level config and cross-reference them from .mcp.json

explanation: .mcp.json supports ${VARIABLE} environment-variable expansion, so the committed config references a variable while the actual secret lives in each developer's local environment — version-controllable yet secret-free. Hardcoding (A) leaks secrets into git; a separate secrets file (C) is non-standard; cross-file references between user config and .mcp.json (D) aren't a supported mechanism.

text: A review system for a 14-file pull request shows inconsistent depth across files, missed bugs in complex files, and contradictory feedback between files. What architecture best addresses this?

options.text: One file per subagent, reviewed in parallel with no cross-file coordination
Increase the context window to include all 14 files at once
Per-file local analysis passes, followed by a separate cross-file integration pass
A single long review session with instructions to keep depth consistent

explanation: Reviewing each file in its own focused pass fixes attention dilution (consistent depth, fewer missed bugs), and a dedicated cross-file integration pass catches interactions like incompatible API changes. Parallel per-file with no coordination (A) misses cross-file issues; a bigger context window (B) doesn't solve dilution; one long session (D) reproduces the original inconsistency.

text: A code-review system prompt must cover security vulnerabilities, code style, and documentation completeness, but natural-language paragraphs lead to inconsistent rule application. Which structuring technique most improves consistency?

options.text: Use XML tags to structure each concern area with explicit criteria
Number each rule and add a 1–10 priority ranking
Write all rules as a single bulleted paragraph for easy scanning
Create separate system prompts per concern and run three independent reviews

explanation: XML-tagged sections (e.g. <security_rules>, <style_rules>, <documentation_rules>) give Claude clear structural boundaries and reduce cross-contamination between concern areas — a well-established prompting practice. Numbering with priorities (B) adds ordering but not separation; a single bulleted blob (C) is still unstructured; three separate reviews (D) triples cost and doesn't improve within-review consistency.

text: A support agent's customer asks to speak with a human manager after one failed troubleshooting attempt. The agent has more troubleshooting steps it could try. What should it do?

options.text: Attempt the remaining troubleshooting steps since it might resolve the issue
Honor the customer's explicit request for a human immediately
Ask whether they'd like to try one more step before escalating
Escalate, but first run diagnostics to give the human context

explanation: An explicit request for a human is a hard escalation trigger and should be honored immediately — overriding it to keep troubleshooting damages trust. Continuing (A) ignores the request; asking to try one more step (C) delays the requested escalation; gating escalation on diagnostics (D) also introduces delay. (Attaching context to the handoff is good — but not as a reason to defer the escalation itself.)

text: Using the Claude Agent SDK (Python), a developer wants to load project-level configuration — skills, CLAUDE.md memory, and custom commands. How should they configure the query() call?

options.text: Pass config_path pointing to the project's .claude directory
Use the --settings CLI flag to point at the config files
Manually read and inject CLAUDE.md into the system prompt
Set setting_sources to include 'project' in ClaudeAgentOptions

explanation: The Agent SDK loads project-level skills, slash commands, and memory when you opt into project setting sources via ClaudeAgentOptions (setting_sources including 'project'). A config_path parameter (A) isn't the mechanism; a CLI flag (B) isn't an SDK option; manually injecting CLAUDE.md (C) is partial and misses skills/commands. (Confirm the exact option name against the current SDK.)

text: For large batch reports, Claude sometimes produces very detailed thinking traces that increase time and cost even on straightforward extraction. What configuration helps optimize this?

options.text: Disable extended thinking entirely for all batch processing
Set effort to 'low' for batch extraction so Claude reduces thinking depth on simple items
Reduce the context window to force shorter thinking traces
Set max_tokens low to truncate lengthy thinking traces

explanation: The effort parameter is a behavioral dial across thinking, tool calls, and verbosity; 'low' makes the model far more economical on simple items while still allowing more thinking when genuinely needed. Disabling thinking entirely (A) removes its benefit on the harder items; shrinking context (C) doesn't target thinking length; a low max_tokens (D) truncates the final output rather than reducing thinking.

text: An agentic system using prompt caching sees cache hits drop to 0% whenever the tool definitions change, even though the rest of the prompt is identical. What explains this?

options.text: Tool definitions aren't part of the cache, so something else must be the cause
Tool definitions sit at the top of the cache hierarchy; changing them invalidates the tools, system, and messages layers
Caching requires a minimum of 4096 tokens of tool definitions
The 5-minute cache TTL expired between calls

explanation: Prompt caching is a prefix match with the render order tools → system → messages. Tools are first, so any change to tool definitions invalidates everything after them — the cached system prompt and messages included — dropping hits to zero. Tools are part of the cache (A is wrong); there's no tool-specific 4096-token rule (C); TTL expiry (D) wouldn't correlate specifically with tool-definition changes.
